lesson.introduction: You've learned about percentages, profit/loss, and simple interest in your Class 8 Math. But did you know these exact formulas run real bank accounts, grocery store discounts, and your parents' salary slips? Let's connect your classroom math to the money world around you.

sections.title: Profit and Loss → Running a Business
sections.content: When a shopkeeper buys a product for ₹80 and sells it for ₹100, the profit is ₹20 — that's 25% profit margin. Every business from your local kirana store to Amazon uses exactly this math. A startup founder calculates the 'gross margin' using the same formula you use for profit percentage.
sections.realWorldConnection: Profit % = (SP - CP) / CP × 100. Flipkart's gross margin in 2023 was about 28% — calculated using this same formula.
sections.keyInsight: Your Chapter 8 profit/loss formula IS the foundation of business accounting.

sections.title: Simple Interest → Bank Savings Accounts
sections.content: If you deposit ₹10,000 in a savings account at 4% per annum, after 2 years you get ₹800 as interest. This is exactly Simple Interest (SI = PRT/100). Every savings account, FD (Fixed Deposit), and loan uses this or compound interest.
sections.realWorldConnection: SBI savings accounts currently offer 2.70% p.a. for balances below ₹10 lakhs. Use SI formula to calculate your annual interest.
sections.keyInsight: Banks pay you for depositing money — and charge you for loans — using your SI/CI formulas.

sections.title: Comparing Quantities → GST and Discounts
sections.content: When you buy a ₹500 book and get 20% discount, you pay ₹400. Then GST of 18% is added = ₹72. Total = ₹472. This is real-world percentage chaining — the same skill you practice in data handling and comparing quantities chapters.
sections.realWorldConnection: Every invoice in India now has GST calculated using percentages. Learning to read a bill requires exactly your Class 8 math skills.
sections.keyInsight: GST = Government's percentage tax. Your percentage chapter directly explains every product bill.

sections.title: Direct and Inverse Proportion → Budgeting
sections.content: If 5 kg of rice costs ₹250, then 8 kg costs ₹400. This is direct proportion — a core skill in budget planning. When you earn more, you can save more (direct proportion). When the price rises, you can buy less with the same money (inverse proportion).
sections.realWorldConnection: A household budget uses proportion calculations daily — how much dal for 4 people vs 6 people, scaling recipes, calculating petrol costs per km.
sections.keyInsight: Proportion is the heart of all scaling problems — from cooking to company budgets.

practicalAddOn.title: ₹500 Experiment: Track Your Money for 2 Weeks
practicalAddOn.description: Take ₹500 (pocket money or a hypothetical amount). Track every ₹ spent and saved over 2 weeks. Calculate: What % went to food? Transport? Entertainment? Savings? This is real budgeting.
steps: Day 1: Open a notebook or use a free app like Walnut to log expenses
Week 1: Record every expense, no matter how small
Week 2: Calculate % for each category using your profit/percentage formulas
Final: Compare actual spending vs what you wished you'd spent — this is variance analysis

agenticChallenge.title: Mini Business Simulation
agenticChallenge.description: Simulate running a small business for one month using only Class 8 math. Start with ₹1,000 capital, buy products (calculate cost price), set selling price for 25% profit, track losses, and compute net profit/loss at month end.
agenticChallenge.hint: Use a spreadsheet (Excel or Google Sheets) — even a 5×5 table works. This is how a real P&L (Profit and Loss) statement works.
agenticChallenge.realWorldConnection: Every startup founder and shopkeeper does exactly this calculation daily.
